name: event
summary: Domain event announcing a state change, with a stable runtime ID and typed payload.
description: Creates an event class with a unique ID
problem: Something meaningful just happened in your domain (a user signed up, an order shipped, a payout settled) and you want to broadcast that fact so other parts of the system can react without being directly coupled to the producer.
appliesWhen: A meaningful state change just happened that other code might want to know about
You want the producer to stay ignorant of who consumes the signal
You expect zero or more listeners to react to this fact
The fact is part of your domain vocabulary, not just plumbing
avoidWhen: Only one consumer will ever care — call it directly
You need a return value from the consumer — events are fire-and-forget
You need ordered, multi-step processing — use an explicit pipeline or saga
The fact is implementation detail, not domain-relevant
examples: Broadcast that an order was placed
Announce when a payout has settled
Fire an event when a deployment finishes
tradeoffs: One new class. Strong decoupling but harder to trace at runtime — you have to inspect the listener registry to know what reacts. Use `phpnomad inspect:di` to surface the wiring.
outputs: A {name} event class implementing the Event interface
A static getId() method returning {eventId} as the runtime identifier
postApply: Add typed properties for the event's payload (orderId, userEmail, etc.)
Scaffold listeners with phpnomad/listener if you have consumers ready
Dispatch the event from wherever the fact actually happens

name.aiHint: Past-tense PascalCase verb describing what happened (UserCreated, not CreateUser; OrderPlaced, not PlaceOrder). The fact, not the action.

eventId.aiHint: Lowercase, dot-separated, namespace-prefixed. Match the class name in past tense (OrderPlaced -> order.placed). Must be unique across the project.
